formulas.context_text: e, if $ M $ is finite, the expected truncated mean overestimates the expected standardized selection differential. For a unit normal distribution, $ \mu_{y_p} = \varphi(y_p)/p = \bar{\nu} $, yielding $$ E\left[\overline{\imath}_{(N,M)}\right]\simeq\overline{\imath}-\left[\frac{M-N}{2N(M+1)}\right]\frac{1}{\overline{\imath}}=\overline{\imath}-\left[\frac{1-p}{2p(M+1)}\right]\frac{1}{\overline{\imath}} $$ (14.4b) where $ \bar{\tau} $ is given by Equation 14.3a, using $ p = N/M $. Lindgren and Nilsson (1985) found this approximation to be quite accurate for $ N \geq 5 $. Bulmer (1980) suggested an alternative

formulas.label: Formula 14.5

formulas.context_text: e, all have essentially the same value, and hence $ n \sim 1 $. If a total of M individuals is sampled, with n individuals per family, then Burrows's correction (Equation 14.4b) is modified to become $$ \begin{align*}E\left[\bar\imath_{(N,M)}\right]\simeq\bar\imath-\left[{1-p\over2p(M+1)(1-\tau+\tau/n)}\right]{1\over\bar\imath}\end{align*} $$ (14.5) where $ \tau $ is the intraclass correlation of family members. This important result for certain types of family selection is revisited in more detail in Chapter 21.

formulas.context_text: nes that $ p(z) $ is a monotonically increasing function of z, approaching 0 for low values and 1 for high values. One reasonable candidate that satisfies these requirements is the logistic function, $$ \ell(z)=\frac{\exp(z)}{1+\exp(z)}=\frac{1}{1+\exp(-z)} $$ (14.10a) with $ \ell(z) \simeq 0 $ for $ z \ll -1, \simeq 1 $ for $ z \gg 1 $, and $ \ell(0) = 1/2 $. formulas.label: Formula 14.16
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: A natural way to model such traits is to use the Poisson distribution, where the probability of observing a trait value of k is given by $$ \Pr(y=k)=e^{-\lambda}\frac{\lambda^{k}}{k!} $$ (14.16) where $ \lambda = E[y] $ is the expected value of the trait, which is constrained to $ \lambda > 0 $. Motivated by the previous treatment of binary traits, one might imagine some underlying liability

formulas.label: Formula 14.17a
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: ry traits, one might imagine some underlying liability value, $ z $, with additive effects (Equation 14.14a) mapped onto a mean value, $ \lambda $, constrained to be positive. For example, we can use $$ \lambda=\exp(z) $$ (14.17a) ensuring that $\lambda > 0$ for all $z$ and hence is a proper expectation for a Poisson. In the context of generalized linear models, $g(z) = \exp(z)$, so that the link function $g^{-1}(z)$ is simply

formulas.label: Formula 14.17b
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: oper expectation for a Poisson. In the context of generalized linear models, $g(z) = \exp(z)$, so that the link function $g^{-1}(z)$ is simply $\ln(z)$, as $g^{-1}[g(z)] = \ln[\exp(z)] = z$, yielding $$ \ln(\lambda)=z=\mu+\sum\beta_{k}x_{k,i}+A_{i}+e_{i} $$ (14.17b)

formulas.label: Formula 14.18
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: The resulting mean trait value in a population under the log-linear model becomes $$ \begin{aligned}E[y]&=E[\lambda]=E[\exp(z)]\\&=E[\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(A)\cdot\exp(e)]\\&=\exp(\mu)\cdot E[\exp(A)]\cdot E[\exp(e)]\\ \end{aligned} $$ (14.18) where the last step follows because (by construction) A and e are uncorrelated (and thus independent if they are bivariate normal, as is usually assumed), while $ \mu $ is a constant. To compute thes

formulas.label: Formula 14.19a

formulas.context_text: stant. To compute these expectations, recall that the expression $ E[e^{tx}] $ is the moment-generating function of the random variable, x (Johnson and Kotz 1970a). For $ x \sim N(\mu, \sigma^{2}) $, $$ E\left[e^{tx}\right]=\exp\left(\mu t+\frac{\sigma^{2}}{2}t\right) $$ (14.19a)

formulas.label: Formula 14.19b

formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS

formulas.label: Formula 14.20a
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: Substituting into Equation 14.18 shows that the expected mean trait value is a function of both the mean, $ \mu $, and variance, $ \sigma_{z}^{2} $, of the underlying liability value, with $$ E[y]=\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp\left(\frac{\sigma_{A}^{2}+\sigma_{e}^{2}}{2}\right)=\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2) $$ (14.20a)

formulas.label: Formula 14.20b

However, Equation 14.20a shows that if we condition on the breeding value being $A$, the conditional mean now becomes $\mu + A$ and the variance, $\sigma_A^2$, vanishes, leaving $$ E[y\mid A]=\exp(\mu+A)\cdot\exp\left(\frac{0+\sigma_{e}^{2}}{2}\right)=\exp(\mu+A)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{e}^{2}/2) $$ (14.20b)

formulas.label: Formula 14.21
formulas.section: Short-term Changes in the Mean: 2. Truncation and Threshold Selection
formulas.subsection: RESPONSE IN DISCRETE TRAITS: POISSON-DISTRIBUTED CHARACTERS
formulas.context_text: e same variance, but now the mean is shifted to $ \mu + h^{2}S $ (where S is the selection differential on the liability scale). Applying Equation 14.20a, the response on the phenotypic scale becomes $$ \begin{aligned}R&=E[y_{t+1}]-E[y_{t}]\\&=\left[\exp(\mu+h^{2}S)-\exp(\mu)\right]\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\&=\left[\exp(h^{2}S)-1\right]\cdot\exp(\mu)\cdot\exp(\sigma_{z}^{2}/2)\\&=\left[\exp(h^{2}S)-1\right]\cdot E(y_{t})\\ \end{aligned} $$ (14.21)
